old_claim.note: Galleon Ship Design provides a capability that enables this technology without being the only possible path.

new_claim.note: Petty's double-bottomed vessels were sailing vessels; the Royal Society record describes examination of the structure and sailing of the new ship.

source_shape.source_locator: Royal Society Picture Library catalog entry for Double-bottomed ship: the object history records a January 28, 1663 committee report on the structure and sailing of Sir William Petty's new ship.
source_shape.source_claim_summary: The source identifies Petty's double-bottomed vessel as a ship whose structure and sailing performance were examined by the Royal Society.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: This supports a direct sailing prerequisite because the scoped technology is an experimental sailing vessel; a galleon-specific lineage is not evidenced for Petty's paired-hull design.

ontology_before: Double-Hulled Ship Designs depended on galleon_ship_design, implying a Spanish-style oceanic war-cargo ship lineage enabled the hull experiment.
ontology_after: Petty Double-Bottomed Ship depends on sailing as the broad operating practice evidenced for the documented paired-hull vessel.
invariant_preserved_or_changed: Changed: double_hulled_ship_designs now depends on sailing and no longer directly depends on galleon_ship_design.
would_reject_if: The node were broadened back to galleon-era oceangoing ship design rather than Petty's double-bottomed experiments.
A stronger source showed Petty's 1662-1664 vessels required galleon ship design specifically.
The sailing node were narrowed to exclude experimental early modern sailing vessels.
short_reason: The source-backed operating capability is sailing, not galleon design.
